title: Server / Systems Administration
description: Cover the operational basics needed to troubleshoot hosts, services, and routine production issues.

reviewer.summary: For sysadmin interviews, you do not need to look like a career ops engineer. You need to show calm troubleshooting, Linux familiarity, and the ability to inspect evidence before acting.
interviewFocus: Do you inspect service state before changing things?
Can you navigate logs, files, processes, and permissions confidently?
Can you explain a safe troubleshooting sequence?
Do you understand common Linux operational primitives?

sections.title: Must Know In Time For Interviews
points: Know how to inspect a process, a service, file permissions, disk usage, and recent logs.
Understand the difference between symptom, evidence, and root cause.
Be able to explain why rebooting first is usually sloppy troubleshooting.

sections.title: Suggested Crash-Course Sequence
points: Start with shell navigation and filesystem permissions.
Then learn processes, services, and logs, followed by monitoring and troubleshooting flow.
Treat package management and mounts as supporting topics rather than the first thing to memorize.

sections.title: What Usually Fails People
points: Changing the system before collecting evidence.
Confusing file ownership issues with application logic bugs.
Giving hand-wavy answers with no concrete commands or checks.

chapters.title: Linux Shell Basics
chapters.focus: Move confidently through a Unix-like system.

chapters.title: Files & Permissions
chapters.focus: Read, write, own, and protect files correctly.

chapters.title: Processes & Services
chapters.focus: Inspect, stop, restart, and diagnose running programs.

chapters.title: Package Management
chapters.focus: Install and update software with intent.

chapters.title: Logs & Debugging
chapters.focus: Use logs to form and test operational hypotheses.

chapters.title: Storage & Mounts
chapters.focus: Understand disks, space pressure, and mount points.

chapters.title: Monitoring
chapters.focus: Know what healthy vs unhealthy looks like.

chapters.title: Troubleshooting
chapters.focus: Work from symptom to root cause methodically.

questions.prompt: A web service is down after deploy. What is the strongest first operational move?

choices.label: Check service status and recent logs before taking larger action
Delete the logs to reduce noise
Upgrade all packages in case a dependency is broken

questions.explanation: Strong operators inspect service state and evidence first. Rebooting before inspection destroys signal and widens the blast radius.

questions.prompt: Which issues are commonly caused by incorrect file permissions?

choices.label: An app cannot read its config file
A non-root user cannot write to a deployment directory
A service account can read secrets it should not see

questions.explanation: Permission mistakes can block legitimate access or expose sensitive files. DNS timeouts are a different class of problem.
